text: The robotic surgical system is docked on the patient's right lateral side, positioning the instruments for the incisional flank hernia repair.
The surgeon uses cautery to remove small adhesions, revealing the extent of the flank hernia.

The surgeon observes omentum attached to the inferior portion of the hernia, opting to leave it in place, which may affect the creation of a full preperitoneal flap and potentially require using the omentum for coverage.
The surgeon begins creating a preperitoneal flap at the site of the flank hernia, located near the semilunar line.
The surgeon positions the ports just lateral to the midline, preparing for further surgical access.
The surgeon adjusts the right lower quadrant port, which was initially placed too close to the surgical site.
The surgeon has successfully dissected the flap, revealing a hernia that appears elongated and positioned more proximally and medially.
The surgeon begins creating a surgical flap, initiating the dissection process to expose the underlying hernia.
The surgeon performs dissection above and below the hernia sac, aiming to encircle it completely and achieve the 'volcano sign' before proceeding with further intervention.
The surgeon continues to lower the peritoneum, revealing the underlying transversalis fascia.
The surgeon observes the attenuated fascia, noting its thinner appearance than anticipated during the procedure.
The surgeon observes the hernia sacs becoming very thin and proceeds to dissect both cephalad and caudad to the hernia, aiming to encircle the structure completely.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the true preperitoneal plane, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon easily separates the retroperitoneal fat from the transversalis fascia laterally, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the hernia sac, which is visible in the surgical field, but inadvertently creates a hole in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon attempts to recruit and mobilize as much of the hernia sac as possible, focusing on the thin sacs visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a non-coated piece of mesh, ensuring it provides full coverage over the hernia site.
The lateral portion of the hernia becomes visible as the surgeon continues to expose the area, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon navigates back into the preperitoneal plane laterally, despite encountering a few holes during the procedure.
The surgeon has maneuvered completely around the hernia sac and is attempting to recruit and gather as much of the sac tissue as possible.
The surgeon positions a non-coated piece of mesh over the hernia site, aiming for a five-centimeter overlap on all sides to ensure full coverage.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, removing retroperitoneal fat within the preperitoneal plane, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the caudal side of the surgical field, with visible difficulty due to the proximity of their right arm to the area being worked on.
The surgeon observes the distinct layers of the abdominal wall, which are clearly visible during the dissection.
The transversalis fascia is visible, appearing as a distinct layer separate from the surrounding abdominal wall structures.
The surgeon observes the internal and external oblique layers of the abdominal wall coming together smoothly during the dissection.
The surgeon uses a permanent OV lock suture to approximate the internal and external oblique complex, visually aligning the layers of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon takes bites of the pseudo hernia sac using sutures, aiming to prevent seroma formation by securing the tissue.
The surgeon prepares to use a six-inch permanent V-lock suture to close the transversalis layer, following a similar technique as previously demonstrated.
The surgeon observes the attenuation of the transversalis fascia, which is visible closer to the camera.
The surgeon closes the true defect in the transversalis fascia, then evaluates the area to determine if additional sutures are needed to re-approximate the attenuated fascia.
The surgeon assesses the amount of transversalis muscle taken during the suturing process, noting that a larger portion may have been included than intended.
The transversalis fascia bulges slightly and begins to bleed, indicating tissue response during the procedure.
The surgeon flips the camera to provide a clearer view of the attenuated transversalis fascia, revealing its trajectory distinct from the previous suturing path.
The surgeon prepares to use an OV lock suture, likely to re-approximate the attenuated transversalis fascia as previously mentioned.
The surgeon selects a 15 by 15 cm circular, soft, macroporous Bard mesh for placement.
The surgeon measures the surgical space, confirming it is approximately seven centimeters in size.
The surgeon introduces a mesh into the surgical field and lays it out flat over the prepared area.
The surgeon centers the marked mesh over the suture line, aligning the middle mark with the surgical site.
The surgeon ensures the mesh fits well over the surgical area, smoothing out any wrinkles to achieve a flat and even placement.
The surgeon sutures the mesh into place, ensuring it is securely attached to the underlying tissue.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture, likely preparing to secure the mesh in place over the suture line.
The surgeon sutures the material into cardinal points, ensuring precise alignment over the suture line marked in the previous step.
The surgeon places a suture in the center of the surgical site, ensuring alignment and tension for proper fit.
The surgeon places four sutures around the perimeter of the surgical site to prevent sliding, using a 2-0 Vicryl suture material.
The surgeon closes the flap over the mesh on the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring it is securely positioned.
The surgeon uses leftover Vicryl suture material to secure the mesh in place, preparing to close the previously identified large hole.
The surgeon uses a purse string suture technique to close a visible hole in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 suture to close a visible hole in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the different layers of the abdominal wall in a flank hernia, focusing on closing both the anterior and posterior sheath for repair.
